allergens: may contain dairy if cream or butter is added

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 250 ml (one bowl), equivalent to 250 g, which is typical for a starter or light meal soup portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Carrot soup, homemade / vegetable soup, prepared with water; supplemented with ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods for carrot and ginger composition
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / ICMR-National Institute of Nutrition
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for carrot and ginger soup exists in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for carrot soup (FDC ID 172945) and ICMR-NIN carrot composition data, adjusted for the addition of ginger, cumin, and a small amount of fat. Input AI-generated values were cross-checked against these sources and found to be within plausible range. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 0.5 g per 100 g based on typical oil/butter content in a lightly enriched vegetable soup.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on carrot (cooked, pureed) and vegetable soup entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for carrot and ginger soup. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on: (1) cooked carrot GI of approximately 39–47 per University of Sydney GI Database; (2) dilution effect of water-based soup preparation; (3) presence of dietary fat and fibre which slow gastric emptying and reduce glycemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (40 × 16.25 g) / 100 = 6.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Moderate sodium content (150 mg/100 g) offset by high potassium (240 mg/100 g); favourable Na:K ratio
hypertension.guidance: Carrot and ginger soup provides a beneficial potassium-to-sodium ratio that supports blood pressure regulation; however, individuals with hypertension should monitor total sodium intake, particularly if the soup is prepared with salted stock or added table salt, and should aim to keep a single serving below 600 mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40), moderate dietary fibre (1.8 g/100 g), and bioactive compounds in ginger that may support insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low GI of approximately 40 and a glycemic load of 6.5 per 250 ml serving, carrot and ginger soup is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions; the fibre content slows glucose absorption, and ginger's gingerols have been associated with improved insulin sensitivity in clinical studies.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High beta-carotene and antioxidant content, low saturated fat (~0.5 g/100 g), and anti-inflammatory properties of ginger
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Carrot and ginger soup is a heart-friendly option due to its low saturated fat content, high beta-carotene (a precursor to vitamin A with antioxidant activity), and the anti-inflammatory gingerols in ginger; individuals with cardiovascular disease should ensure the soup is prepared with minimal added salt and unsaturated fat sources such as olive oil.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory gingerols and antioxidant beta-carotene from carrots
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ginger contains gingerols and shogaols with demonstrated anti-inflammatory properties relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management; regular consumption as part of an anti-inflammatory diet may provide symptomatic benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Carrot and Ginger Soup is a smooth, pureed vegetable soup made primarily from carrots and fresh ginger, prepared by simmering the vegetables in stock and blending until velvety. In the Indian-fusion context, it is typically seasoned with cumin, black pepper, and sometimes a small amount of cream or coconut milk to add richness. The flavour profile combines the natural sweetness of carrots with the sharp, warming heat of ginger, producing a comforting and aromatic soup. Nutritionally, it is a low-calorie, low-fat preparation (approximately 45 kcal per 100 g) that is rich in beta-carotene — the precursor to vitamin A — providing approximately 325 mcg RAE per 100 g, which supports eye health and immune function. The dietary fibre content (1.8 g/100 g) and low glycemic index (~40) make it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or seeking weight management support. Ginger contributes bioactive compounds including gingerols and shogaols, which have anti-inflammatory and digestive properties. The soup is commonly served as a starter or light meal at lunch or dinner and is particularly popular as a winter comfort food. It is naturally gluten-free and vegetarian, and can be made vegan by omitting cream. Clinically, it is appropriate for individuals with hypertension (favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio), cardiovascular disease (low saturated fat, high antioxidants), and rheumatoid arthritis (anti-inflammatory ginger).
common_user_queries: Is carrot and ginger soup good for diabetes?
How many calories in carrot and ginger soup?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for carrot and ginger soup exists in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from the closest approved-source equivalents (USDA carrot soup entry and ICMR-NIN carrot composition data) and cross-checked against the AI-generated input values, which fall within plausible ranges. GI is an analogous estimate based on cooked carrot GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: these are confirmed zero for a plant-based vegetable soup with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant foods and vitamin D is naturally zero in this preparation. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as this is a minimally processed vegetable soup with no hydrogenated fats. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based preparation. 2. water_g set to -1: detailed moisture data for this specific Indian-fusion carrot and ginger soup preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or ICMR-NIN as of 2024. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae: the input value of 650 mcg was reported as retinol equivalents (RE) from the enriched JSON; this has been converted to approximately 325 mcg RAE (1 mcg beta-carotene from food = 0.5 mcg RAE per IOM conversion factors) to align with the RAE standard used in the RESULT template. 4. saturated_fat_g (0.5 g), monounsaturated_fat_g (0.9 g), and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.5 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for a lightly oil-enriched vegetable soup; these values are not reported in the original input and are flagged as estimated. 5. Portions in the input contained three identical duplicate entries; deduplicated to a single serving of 250 g. 6. GI glycemic load per serving calculated as (40 × 16.25 g available carbohydrate per 250 g serving) / 100 = 6.5.
